mappings.rationaleAI: Directly covers the development of microscopes from Leeuwenhoek's single-lens instruments through compound microscopes to electron and scanning probe microscopes.

Covers the measurement of time from ancient sundials through mechanical clocks, pendulum clocks, marine chronometers, to atomic clocks, directly relevant to standards of time measurement and devices for measuring time.
Longitude measurement covers Harrison's marine chronometers and the lunar distance method, directly relevant to navigational techniques and instruments for measuring position by angulation.
Chromatography is an analytical technique using specialized laboratory equipment for separating mixtures, directly relevant to chromatographs as research instruments.
Optics covers lenses, telescopes, microscopes, fibre optics, and lasers, directly relevant to instruments for observing phenomena and optical measurement devices.

Meteorology covers barometers, thermometers, satellite and radar technology for atmospheric observation, directly relevant to instruments for measuring pressure and meteorological measurement systems.
The Antikythera Mechanism is an ancient astronomical observation device with a complex gear system for predicting eclipses and tracking celestial bodies.
Magnetism covers compasses from lodestones to modern applications, directly relevant to magnetic compasses as instruments for measuring position and direction finding.
Radiation covers the electromagnetic spectrum and detection methods, relevant to instruments for measuring ionizing radiation and spectroscopic observation.
Semiconductor physics enabling transistors and integrated circuits is foundational to modern instrumentation and electronic control systems.
William and Caroline Herschel constructed the largest telescopes of their age and mapped nebulae and the Milky Way, directly relevant to telescopic observation and astronomical observations.

Astronomy and Empire covers how navigation, timekeeping, and cartography drove astronomical research and the role of observatories, directly relevant to major systems of measurement and navigational techniques.
Absolute zero covers the history of thermometry and low-temperature physics, relevant to devices for measuring temperature.
The scientific method covers principles of observation, experiment, and measurement validation, relevant to the theory of measurement.
